156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.

When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:

The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from

11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
15	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
16	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
17	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
18	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
19	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
20	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
21	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
22	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
23	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
24	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
25	
26	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
27	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
28	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
29	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
30	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
31	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
32	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
33	\[
34	  \begin{array}{ccc}
35	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
36	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
37	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
38	  \end{array}
39	\]
40	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
41	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
42	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
43	
44	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
45	
46	\begin{definition}
47

50	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
52	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
53	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
54	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
55	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
56	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
57	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
58	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
59	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
60	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
61	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
62	  % the unit ideal, see

65	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
66	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
67	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
68	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
69	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
70	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
71	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
72	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
73	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
74	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
75	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
76	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
77	  \[
78	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
79	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
80	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
81	  \]
82	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
83	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
84	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
85	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
86	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
87	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
88	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
89	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
90	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
91	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
92	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
93	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
94	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
95	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).

162	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
163	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
164	
165	It is convenient to factor the construction of
166	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
167	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
168	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
169	
170	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
171	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
172	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
173	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
174	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
175	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
176	\[
177	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
178	    \;=\;
179	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
180	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
181	\]
182	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
183	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
184	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
185	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
186	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
187	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
188	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
189	
190	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
191	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
192	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
193	\[
194	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
195	  \qquad
196	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
197	\]
198	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
199	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
200	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
201	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
202	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
203	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
204	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
205	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
206	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
207	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
208	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
209	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
210	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
211	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
212	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
213	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
214	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
215	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
216	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
217	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
218	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
219	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
220	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
221	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
222	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
223	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
224	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
225	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
226	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
227	
228	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
229	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
230	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
231	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
232	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
233	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
234	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
235	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
236	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
237	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
238	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
239	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
240	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
241	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
242	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
243	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
244	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
245	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
246	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
247	
248	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
249	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
250	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
251	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
252	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
253	
254	\begin{definition}\leanok
255	[Arrow of an affine cover]
256	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
257	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
258	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
259	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
260	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
261	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
262	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
263	  apply.
264	\end{definition}
265	
266	\begin{definition}\leanok
267	[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
268	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
269	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve}
270	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
271	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
272	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
273	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
274	  \[
275	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
276	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
277	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
278	  \]
279	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
280	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
281	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
282	\end{definition}
283	
284	\begin{definition}\leanok
285	[Push--pull object map]
286	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
287	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
288	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
289	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
290	  \[
291	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
292	  \]
293	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
294	\end{definition}
295	
296	\begin{definition}\leanok
297	[Push--pull comparison map]
298	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
299	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap}
300	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
301	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
302	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
303	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
304	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
305	  \[
306	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
307	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
308	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
309	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
310	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
311	      \;=\;
312	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
313	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
314	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
315	  \]
316	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
317	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
318	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
319	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
320	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
321	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
322	\end{definition}
323	
324	\begin{lemma}
325	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
326	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
327	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
328	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
329	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
330	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
331	\end{lemma}
332	
333	\begin{proof}
334	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
335	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
336	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
337	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
338	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
339	\end{proof}
340	
341	\begin{lemma}
342	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
343	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
344	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp}
345	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
346	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
347	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
348	  \[
349	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
350	  \]
351	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
352	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
353	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
354	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
355	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
356	\end{lemma}
357	
358	\begin{proof}
359	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
360	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
361	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
362	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
363	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
364	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
365	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
366	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
367	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
368	\end{proof}
369	
370	\begin{lemma}
371	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
372	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
373	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
374	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
375	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
376	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
377	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
378	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
379	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
380	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
381	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
382	  \[
383	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
384	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
385	    \;=\;
386	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
387	  \]
388	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
389	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
390	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
391	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
392	\end{lemma}
393	
394	\begin{proof}
395	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
396	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
397	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
398	\end{proof}
399	
400	\begin{lemma}
401	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
402	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
403	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
404	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
405	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
406	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
407	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
408	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
409	  collapses to the transport-light form
410	  \[
411	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
412	      \;\circ\; e_2
413	    \;=\;
414	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
415	      \;\circ\; e_3,
416	  \]
417	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
418	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
419	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
420	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
421	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
422	  composition (pentagon) law.
423	\end{lemma}
424	
425	\begin{proof}
426	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
427	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
428	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
429	\end{proof}

431	\begin{definition}
432	[Push--pull functor]
433	  \label{def:push_pull_functor}
434	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullFunctor}
435	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
436	  The \emph{push--pull functor}
437	  \[
438	    G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
439	  \]
440	  assembling the object map \(p \mapsto p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F}\) of
441	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_obj} and the comparison morphism map of
442	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a genuine functor. Functoriality is exactly the two
443	  laws established above: the identity law
444	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} and the composition law Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_comp}. The
445	  functor is contravariant in the over-category \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\); it is the lift of the
446	  geometric backbone (1) to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described as step (2) above.
447	\end{definition}

449	\begin{definition}
450	[{\v C}ech nerve cosimplicial object]
451	  \label{def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial}
452	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechNerveCosimplicial}
453	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor}
454	  The (non-augmented) cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module obtained by transporting the
455	  geometric {\v C}ech backbone in \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\) of
456	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} through the push--pull functor
457	  \(G\) of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_functor}: degreewise, the \(p\)-th term is the
458	  product over \((i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}\) of the \((p+1)\)-fold-intersection
459	  pushforwards \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\). This is the
460	  underlying cosimplicial object that the {\v C}ech nerve of
461	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} augments by the structure map \(\mathcal{F} \to
462	  \mathcal{C}^0\).
463	\end{definition}

465	\begin{definition}\leanok
466	[Relative {\v C}ech complex from a nerve]
467	  \label{def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
468	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.relativeCechComplexOfNerve}
469	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
470	  Given \(f : X \to S\) and an augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
471	  \(N\), the \emph{relative {\v C}ech cochain complex} in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)
472	  obtained by forgetting the augmentation of \(N\), pushing the resulting
473	  cosimplicial object forward degreewise along \(f\), and taking the
474	  alternating-coface-map cochain complex. This passage uses only the preadditivity
475	  of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) and \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence; it is
476	  the coherence-free plumbing (3) that turns the nerve of
477	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} into the {\v C}ech complex.
478	\end{definition}

1538	\end{proof}
1539	
1540	\begin{lemma}
1541	\label{lemma-cech-spectral-sequence}
1542	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
1543	Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering.
1544	For any sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ there
1545	is a spectral sequence $(E_r, d_r)_{r \geq 0}$ with
1546	$$
1547	E_2^{p, q} = \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))
1548	$$
1549	converging to $H^{p + q}(U, \mathcal{F})$.
1550	This spectral sequence is functorial in $\mathcal{F}$.
1551	\end{lemma}
1552	
1553	\begin{proof}
1554	This is a Grothendieck spectral sequence
1555	(see
1556	Derived Categories, Lemma \ref{derived-lemma-grothendieck-spectral-sequence})
1557	for the functors
1558	$$
1559	i :  \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)
1560	\quad\text{and}\quad
1561	\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, - ) : \textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)
1562	\to \text{Mod}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}.
1563	$$
1564	Namely, we have $\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, i(\mathcal{F})) = \mathcal{F}(U)$
1565	by Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-h0}. We have that $i(\mathcal{I})$ is
1566	{\v C}ech acyclic by Lemma \ref{lemma-injective-trivial-cech}. And we
1567	have that $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, -) = R^p\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -)$
1568	as functors on $\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
1569	by Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-derived-presheaves}.
1570	Putting everything together gives the lemma.
1571	\end{proof}
1572	
1573	\begin{lemma}
1574	\label{lemma-cech-spectral-sequence-application}
1575	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
1576	Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering.
1577	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1578	Assume that $H^i(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$
1579	for all $i > 0$, all $p \geq 0$ and all $i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
1580	Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(U, \mathcal{F})$
1581	as $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-modules.
1582	\end{lemma}
1583	
1584	\begin{proof}
1585	We will use the spectral sequence of
1586	Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-spectral-sequence}.
1587	The assumptions mean that $E_2^{p, q} = 0$ for all $(p, q)$ with
1588	$q \not = 0$. Hence the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$
1589	and the result follows.
1590	\end{proof}
1591	
1592	\begin{lemma}
1593	\label{lemma-ses-cech-h1}
1594	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
1595	Let
1596	$$
1597	0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H} \to 0
1598	$$
1599	be a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1600	Let $U \subset X$ be an open subset.
1601	If there exists a cofinal system of open coverings $\mathcal{U}$
1602	of $U$ such that $\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$,
1603	then the map $\mathcal{G}(U) \to \mathcal{H}(U)$ is
1604	surjective.
1605	\end{lemma}
1606	
1607	\begin{proof}
1608	Take an element $s \in \mathcal{H}(U)$. Choose an open covering
1609	$\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ such that
1610	(a) $\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ and (b)
1611	$s|_{U_i}$ is the image of a section $s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)$.
1612	Since we can certainly find $\mathcal{U}$ such that (b) holds
1613	it follows from the assumptions of the lemma that we can find
1614	$\mathcal{U}$ such that (a) and (b) both hold.
1615	Consider the sections
1616	$$
1617	s_{i_0i_1} = s_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0i_1}} - s_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0i_1}}.
1618	$$
1619	Since $s_i$ lifts $s$ we see that $s_{i_0i_1} \in \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0i_1})$.
1620	By the vanishing of $\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$ we can
1621	find sections $t_i \in \mathcal{F}(U_i)$ such that
1622	$$
1623	s_{i_0i_1} = t_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0i_1}} - t_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0i_1}}.
1624	$$
1625	Then clearly the sections $s_i - t_i$ satisfy the sheaf condition
1626	and glue to a section of $\mathcal{G}$ over $U$ which maps to $s$.
1627	Hence we win.
1628	\end{proof}
1629	
1630	\begin{lemma}
1631	\label{lemma-cech-vanish}
1632	\begin{slogan}
1633	If higher {\v C}ech cohomology of an abelian sheaf vanishes for all open covers,
1634	then higher cohomology vanishes.
1635	\end{slogan}
1636	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
1637	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module such that
1638	$$
1639	\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0
1640	$$
1641	for all $p > 0$ and any open covering $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$
1642	of an open of $X$. Then $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$
1643	and any open $U \subset X$.
1644	\end{lemma}
1645	
1646	\begin{proof}
1647	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf satisfying the assumption of the lemma.
1648	We will indicate this by saying ``$\mathcal{F}$ has vanishing higher
1649	{\v C}ech cohomology for any open covering''.
1650	Choose an embedding $\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I}$ into an
1651	injective $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1652	By Lemma \ref{lemma-injective-trivial-cech} $\mathcal{I}$ has vanishing higher
1653	{\v C}ech cohomology for any open covering.
1654	Let $\mathcal{Q} = \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}$
1655	so that we have a short exact sequence
1656	$$
1657	0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0.
1658	$$
1659	By Lemma \ref{lemma-ses-cech-h1} and our assumptions
1660	this sequence is actually exact as a sequence of presheaves!
1661	In particular we have a long exact sequence of {\v C}ech cohomology
1662	groups for any open covering $\mathcal{U}$, see
1663	Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-delta-functor-presheaves}
1664	for example. This implies that $\mathcal{Q}$ is also an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1665	with vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for all open coverings.
1666	
1667	\medskip\noindent
1668	Next, we look at the long exact cohomology sequence
1669	$$
1670	\xymatrix{
1671	0 \ar[r] &
1672	H^0(U, \mathcal{F}) \ar[r] &
1673	H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \ar[r] &
1674	H^0(U, \mathcal{Q}) \ar[lld] \\
1675	&
1676	H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) \ar[r] &
1677	H^1(U, \mathcal{I}) \ar[r] &
1678	H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) \ar[lld] \\
1679	&
1680	\ldots & \ldots & \ldots \\
1681	}
1682	$$
1683	for any open $U \subset X$. Since $\mathcal{I}$ is injective we
1684	have $H^n(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for $n > 0$ (see
1685	Derived Categories, Lemma \ref{derived-lemma-higher-derived-functors}).
1686	By the above we see that $H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \to H^0(U, \mathcal{Q})$
1687	is surjective and hence $H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$.
1688	Since $\mathcal{F}$ was an arbitrary $\mathcal{O}_X$-module with
1689	vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology we conclude that also
1690	$H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0$ since $\mathcal{Q}$ is another of these
1691	sheaves (see above). By the long exact sequence this in turn implies
1692	that $H^2(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$. And so on and so forth.
1693	\end{proof}
1694	
1695	\begin{lemma}
1696	\label{lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
1697	(Variant of Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-vanish}.)
1698	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
1699	Let $\mathcal{B}$ be a basis for the topology on $X$.
1700	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1701	Assume there exists a set of open coverings $\text{Cov}$
1702	with the following properties:
1703	\begin{enumerate}
1704	\item For every $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
1705	with $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ we have
1706	$U, U_i \in \mathcal{B}$ and every $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \in \mathcal{B}$.
1707	\item For every $U \in \mathcal{B}$ the open coverings of $U$
1708	occurring in $\text{Cov}$ is a cofinal system of open coverings
1709	of $U$.
1710	\item For every $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$ we have
1711	$\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$.
1712	\end{enumerate}
1713	Then $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$ and any $U \in \mathcal{B}$.
1714	\end{lemma}
1715	
1716	\begin{proof}
1717	Let $\mathcal{F}$ and $\text{Cov}$ be as in the lemma.
1718	We will indicate this by saying ``$\mathcal{F}$ has vanishing higher
1719	{\v C}ech cohomology for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$''.
1720	Choose an embedding $\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I}$ into an
1721	injective $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1722	By Lemma \ref{lemma-injective-trivial-cech} $\mathcal{I}$
1723	has vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
1724	Let $\mathcal{Q} = \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}$
1725	so that we have a short exact sequence
1726	$$
1727	0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0.
1728	$$
1729	By Lemma \ref{lemma-ses-cech-h1} and our assumption (2)
1730	this sequence gives rise to an exact sequence
1731	$$
1732	0 \to \mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U) \to 0.
1733	$$
1734	for every $U \in \mathcal{B}$. Hence for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
1735	we get a short exact sequence of {\v C}ech complexes
1736	$$
1737	0 \to
1738	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
1739	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
1740	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to 0
1741	$$
1742	since each term in the {\v C}ech complex is made up out of a product of
1743	values over elements of $\mathcal{B}$ by assumption (1).
1744	In particular we have a long exact sequence of {\v C}ech cohomology
1745	groups for any open covering $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
1746	This implies that $\mathcal{Q}$ is also an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1747	with vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for all
1748	$\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
1749	
1750	\medskip\noindent
1751	Next, we look at the long exact cohomology sequence
1752	$$
1753	\xymatrix{
1754	0 \ar[r] &
1755	H^0(U, \mathcal{F}) \ar[r] &
1756	H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \ar[r] &
1757	H^0(U, \mathcal{Q}) \ar[lld] \\
1758	&
1759	H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) \ar[r] &
1760	H^1(U, \mathcal{I}) \ar[r] &
1761	H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) \ar[lld] \\
1762	&
1763	\ldots & \ldots & \ldots \\
1764	}
1765	$$
1766	for any $U \in \mathcal{B}$. Since $\mathcal{I}$ is injective we
1767	have $H^n(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for $n > 0$ (see
1768	Derived Categories, Lemma \ref{derived-lemma-higher-derived-functors}).
1769	By the above we see that $H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \to H^0(U, \mathcal{Q})$
1770	is surjective and hence $H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$.
1771	Since $\mathcal{F}$ was an arbitrary $\mathcal{O}_X$-module with
1772	vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for all $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
1773	we conclude that also $H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0$ since $\mathcal{Q}$ is
1774	another of these sheaves (see above). By the long exact sequence this in
1775	turn implies that $H^2(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$. And so on and so forth.
1776	\end{proof}
1777	
1778	\begin{lemma}
1779	\label{lemma-pushforward-injective}
1780	Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
1781	Let $\mathcal{I}$ be an injective $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1782	Then
1783	\begin{enumerate}
1784	\item $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{V}, f_*\mathcal{I}) = 0$
1785	for all $p > 0$ and any open covering
1786	$\mathcal{V} : V = \bigcup_{j \in J} V_j$ of $Y$.

/-- A family of elements spanning the unit ideal of `R` gives an affine open cover of `Spec R`. -/
@[simps]
noncomputable
def affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop {R : CommRingCat} {ι : Type*} (s : ι → R)
    (hs : Ideal.span (Set.range s) = ⊤) : (Spec R).AffineOpenCover where
  I₀ := ι
  X i := .of (Localization.Away (s i))
  f i := Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap R (Localization.Away (s i))))
  idx x := by
    have : ∃ i, s i ∉ x.asIdeal := by
      by_contra! h; apply x.2.ne_top; rwa [← top_le_iff, ← hs, Ideal.span_le, Set.range_subset_iff]
    exact this.choose
  covers x := by
    generalize_proofs H
    let i := H.choose
    have := PrimeSpectrum.localization_away_comap_range (Localization.Away (s i)) (s i)
    exact (eq_iff_iff.mp congr(x ∈ $this)).mpr H.choose_spec

/-- Given any open cover `𝓤`, this is an affine open cover which refines it. -/
def OpenCover.fromAffineRefinement {X : Scheme.{u}} (𝓤 : X.OpenCover) :
    𝓤.affineRefinement.openCover ⟶ 𝓤 where
  s₀ j := j.fst
  h₀ j := (𝓤.X j.fst).affineCover.f _

/-- If two global sections agree after restriction to each member of an open cover, then
they agree globally. -/
lemma OpenCover.ext_elem {X : Scheme.{u}} {U : X.Opens} (f g : Γ(X, U)) (𝒰 : X.OpenCover)
    (h : ∀ i : 𝒰.I₀, (𝒰.f i).app U f = (𝒰.f i).app U g) : f = g := by
  fapply TopCat.Sheaf.eq_of_locally_eq' X.sheaf
    (fun i ↦ (𝒰.f (𝒰.idx i)).opensRange ⊓ U) _ (fun _ ↦ homOfLE inf_le_right)
  · intro x hx
    simp only [Opens.iSup_mk, Opens.carrier_eq_coe, Opens.coe_inf, Hom.coe_opensRange, Opens.mem_mk,
      Set.mem_iUnion, Set.mem_inter_iff, Set.mem_range, SetLike.mem_coe, exists_and_right]
    refine ⟨?_, hx⟩
    simpa using ⟨_, 𝒰.covers x⟩

  \label{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}

\begin{lemma}

  % $$

  % then the map $\mathcal{G}(U) \to \mathcal{H}(U)$ is
  % surjective."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
  \texttt{lemma-ses-cech-h1}.}
  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and let
  \[
    0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H} \to 0
  \]
  be a short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Let \(U \subset X\) be
  open. If there is a cofinal system of open coverings \(\mathcal{U}\) of \(U\) with
  \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\), then the map on sections
  \(\mathcal{G}(U) \to \mathcal{H}(U)\) is surjective. Equivalently, the short exact
  sequence is exact on sections over such \(U\).
\end{lemma}

% it follows from the assumptions of the lemma that we can find
% $\mathcal{U}$ such that (a) and (b) both hold.
% Consider the sections
% $$
% s_{i_0i_1} = s_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0i_1}} - s_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0i_1}}.
% $$
% Since $s_i$ lifts $s$ we see that $s_{i_0i_1} \in \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0i_1})$.
% By the vanishing of $\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$ we can
% find sections $t_i \in \mathcal{F}(U_i)$ such that
% $$
% s_{i_0i_1} = t_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0i_1}} - t_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0i_1}}.
% $$
% Then clearly the sections $s_i - t_i$ satisfy the sheaf condition
% and glue to a section of $\mathcal{G}$ over $U$ which maps to $s$.
% Hence we win."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:cech_complex}
  Let \(s \in \mathcal{H}(U)\). Choose an open covering \(\mathcal{U} : U =
  \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) fine enough that (a) \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U},
  \mathcal{F}) = 0\) and (b) each restriction \(s|_{U_i}\) lifts to a section
  \(s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\); both can be arranged simultaneously because the
  coverings with property (a) are cofinal and (b) holds on any sufficiently fine
  cover (surjectivity of \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H}\) is a local condition).

    s_{i_0 i_1} = s_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}} - s_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}}.

  \(1\)-cocycle for \(\mathcal{F}\). By \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\)

\end{proof}

  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.
  % As our basis $\mathcal{B}$ for the topology of $X$ we are going to use
  % the affine opens of $X$.
  % As our set $\text{Cov}$ of open coverings we are going to use the standard
  % open coverings of affine opens of $X$.
  % Next we check that conditions (1), (2) and (3) of
  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
  % hold. Note that the intersection of standard opens in an affine is
  % another standard open. Hence property (1) holds.
  % The coverings form a cofinal system of open coverings of any element
  % of $\mathcal{B}$, see
  % Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-standard-open}.
  % Hence (2) holds.
  % Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
  % Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Lemma
  \texttt{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (as applied in the proof of
  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}).}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\mathcal{B}\) be a basis for its topology, and let a
  set of admissible open covers be given, subject to: (1) \(\mathcal{B}\) is closed
  under finite intersection; (2) for each \(B \in \mathcal{B}\) the admissible covers
  of \(B\) are cofinal among all open covers of \(B\); and (3) for every admissible
  cover \(\mathcal{U}\) of every \(B \in \mathcal{B}\), every quasi-coherent
  \(\mathcal{F}\), and every \(p > 0\), the {\v C}ech cohomology vanishes,
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Then for every \(B \in \mathcal{B}\)
  and every \(p\) the canonical comparison map is an isomorphism
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \cong H^p(B, \mathcal{F})\); in particular,
  under (3), \(H^p(B, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\). This is the
  basis-comparison vehicle that upgrades the standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} to the Serre vanishing of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
  Take \(X\) a scheme, \(\mathcal{B}\) the basis of affine opens, the admissible
  covers the standard affine covers, and \(\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent. Fix an
  affine open \(B = \operatorname{Spec}(A) \in \mathcal{B}\) and a standard cover
  \(\mathcal{U} : B = \bigcup_i D(f_i)\); we show \(H^k(B, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all
  \(k \geq 1\) and that the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison is an isomorphism,
  via the acyclic-resolution route.

  Form the augmented {\v C}ech complex of \(\mathcal{U}\) on \(B\),
  \[
    0 \;\to\; \mathcal{F} \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^0 \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^1 \;\to\; \cdots,
    \qquad
    \mathcal{C}^p \;=\; \prod_{\sigma} (j_\sigma)_*\bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_\sigma}\bigr),
  \]
  the product running over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections \(U_\sigma =
  D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\). By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution} this augmented complex is exact, so
  \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\); its exactness is the
  prime-local contracting homotopy of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}, with no
  sheaf-cohomology input.

  Each term \(\mathcal{C}^p\) is acyclic for the global-sections functor
  \(G = \Gamma(B, -)\). Indeed each \(U_\sigma = D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\) is a basic
  open of \(B\), hence affine, and the higher cohomology of
  \((j_\sigma)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_\sigma})\) on \(B\) is computed by the module-level
  {\v C}ech complex of a standard cover of the affine \(U_\sigma\), which is exact in
  positive degrees by the same contracting homotopy of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} applied one step down to \(U_\sigma\); a product
  of \(G\)-acyclic objects is \(G\)-acyclic. There is no spectral sequence and no
  appeal to sheaf cohomology — only standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing.

  Apply the acyclic-resolution comparison
  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} with \(G = \Gamma(B, -)\) and the
  resolution \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\): for every \(k\),
  \[
    H^k(B, \mathcal{F}) \;\cong\; H^k\bigl(\Gamma(B, \mathcal{C}^\bullet)\bigr)
      \;=\; \check{H}^k(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}).
  \]
  By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} the right-hand {\v C}ech cohomology vanishes
  for \(k \geq 1\), whence \(H^k(B, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\); the comparison
  map \(\check{H}^k(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to H^k(B, \mathcal{F})\) is then an
  isomorphism (both sides vanish for \(k \geq 1\), and in degree \(0\) both equal
  \(\Gamma(B, \mathcal{F})\)). The argument is non-circular: it takes narrowed
  standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}) as input and
  lifts it to affine sheaf vanishing as output, with no use of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}.
\end{proof}

\label{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_eq_cohomology_of_basis}
  \uses{def:cech_complex}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (a variant of \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish}; the
  % basis-comparison criterion for {\v C}ech vs.\ sheaf cohomology), statement and
  % proof (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1695--1776).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(Variant of Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-vanish}.)
  % Let $X$ be a ringed space.
  % Let $\mathcal{B}$ be a basis for the topology on $X$.
  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
  % Assume there exists a set of open coverings $\text{Cov}$
  % with the following properties:
  % \begin{enumerate}
  % \item For every $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
  % with $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ we have
  % $U, U_i \in \mathcal{B}$ and every $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \in \mathcal{B}$.
  % \item For every $U \in \mathcal{B}$ the open coverings of $U$
  % occurring in $\text{Cov}$ is a cofinal system of open coverings
  % of $U$.
  % \item For every $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$ we have
  % $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$.
  % \end{enumerate}
  % Then $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$ and any $U \in \mathcal{B}$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma
  \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.}
  Let \(X\) be a ringed space, let \(\mathcal{B}\) be a basis for its topology, let
  \(\mathcal{F}\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and suppose there is a set
  \(\mathrm{Cov}\) of open coverings such that: (1) every \(\mathcal{U} :
  U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) in \(\mathrm{Cov}\) has \(U, U_i \in \mathcal{B}\) and
  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \in \mathcal{B}\); (2) for each
  \(U \in \mathcal{B}\) the coverings of \(U\) occurring in \(\mathrm{Cov}\) are
  cofinal among all open coverings of \(U\); and (3) for every \(\mathcal{U} \in
  \mathrm{Cov}\) the {\v C}ech cohomology vanishes,
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\). Then
  \[
    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0 \text{ and any }
      U \in \mathcal{B}.
  \]
  This is the basis-comparison vehicle that upgrades the standard-cover {\v C}ech
  vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} to the Serre vanishing of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}: instantiating \(\mathcal{B}\) as the affine
  opens and \(\mathrm{Cov}\) as the standard affine covers, condition (3) is exactly
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Let $\mathcal{F}$ and $\text{Cov}$ be as in the lemma.
% We will indicate this by saying ``$\mathcal{F}$ has vanishing higher
% {\v C}ech cohomology for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$''.
% Choose an embedding $\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I}$ into an
% injective $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
% By Lemma \ref{lemma-injective-trivial-cech} $\mathcal{I}$
% has vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
% Let $\mathcal{Q} = \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}$
% so that we have a short exact sequence
% $$
% 0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0.
% $$
% By Lemma \ref{lemma-ses-cech-h1} and our assumption (2)
% this sequence gives rise to an exact sequence
% $$
% 0 \to \mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U) \to 0.
% $$
% for every $U \in \mathcal{B}$. Hence for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
% we get a short exact sequence of {\v C}ech complexes
% $$
% 0 \to
% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to 0
% $$
% since each term in the {\v C}ech complex is made up out of a product of
% values over elements of $\mathcal{B}$ by assumption (1).
% In particular we have a long exact sequence of {\v C}ech cohomology
% groups for any open covering $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
% This implies that $\mathcal{Q}$ is also an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
% with vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for all
% $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
% Next, we look at the long exact cohomology sequence ...
% for any $U \in \mathcal{B}$. Since $\mathcal{I}$ is injective we
% have $H^n(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for $n > 0$ (see
% Derived Categories, Lemma \ref{derived-lemma-higher-derived-functors}).
% By the above we see that $H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \to H^0(U, \mathcal{Q})$
% is surjective and hence $H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$.
% Since $\mathcal{F}$ was an arbitrary $\mathcal{O}_X$-module with
% vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for all $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
% we conclude that also $H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0$ since $\mathcal{Q}$ is
% another of these sheaves (see above). By the long exact sequence this in
% turn implies that $H^2(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$. And so on and so forth."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:injective_cech_acyclic, lem:ses_cech_h1, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
  Say that an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module has \emph{vanishing higher {\v C}ech
  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)} if condition (3) holds for it. We argue by a
  dimension shift, taking \(\mathcal{F}\) (with vanishing higher {\v C}ech
  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)) as the inductive variable and using no input from
  affine sheaf-cohomology vanishing.

  Embed \(\mathcal{F} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{I}\) into an injective
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\). By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} the injective \(\mathcal{I}\) has
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and every cover, in
  particular for every \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\). Put \(\mathcal{Q} =
  \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}\), giving the short exact sequence
  \[
    0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0.
  \]
  For each \(U \in \mathcal{B}\) the coverings of \(\mathrm{Cov}\) are cofinal among
  coverings of \(U\) (condition (2)) and \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
  0\) for them, so Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1} makes the sequence exact on sections,
  \[
    0 \to \mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U) \to 0,
    \qquad U \in \mathcal{B}.
  \]
  Since by condition (1) every term of a {\v C}ech complex for a cover
  \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\) is a product of values over elements of
  \(\mathcal{B}\), this section-level exactness yields a short exact sequence of
  {\v C}ech complexes
  \[
    0 \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to 0,
  \]
  and hence a long exact sequence of {\v C}ech cohomology. As
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) =
  0\) for \(p > 0\), the long exact sequence forces \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},
  \mathcal{Q}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\): the quotient \(\mathcal{Q}\) again has
  vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\).

  Now run the sheaf-cohomology long exact sequence of \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to
  \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) over a fixed \(U \in \mathcal{B}\). The
  injective \(\mathcal{I}\) has \(H^n(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(n > 0\). The
  section-level surjectivity \(\mathcal{I}(U) = H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \to H^0(U,
  \mathcal{Q}) = \mathcal{Q}(U)\) established above makes the connecting map into
  \(H^1(U, \mathcal{F})\) zero, whence \(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Applying the
  same conclusion to \(\mathcal{Q}\) (another module with vanishing higher {\v C}ech
  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)) gives \(H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0\), and then the
  long exact sequence with \(H^1(U, \mathcal{I}) = H^2(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) forces
  \(H^2(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Iterating the dimension shift — at each stage
  \(\mathcal{Q}\) plays the role of \(\mathcal{F}\) — gives \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) =
  0\) for all \(p > 0\) and every \(U \in \mathcal{B}\).

  The argument never uses affine sheaf-cohomology vanishing
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}); its only {\v C}ech-vanishing input is
  condition (3), which on the affine instantiation is supplied by the standard-cover
  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}. Thus the dependency
  \(\text{affine Serre vanishing} \to \text{this lemma}\) is one-directional and the
  graph is acyclic.
\end{proof}

1073	  (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}).}
1074	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{G}\) an
1075	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For every \(k \geq 0\) the higher direct image
1076	  \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G} = (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{G})\) is the sheaf
1077	  on \(S\) associated to the presheaf
1078	  \[
1079	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
1080	    \qquad V \subseteq S \text{ open},
1081	  \]
1082	  with the evident restriction maps. In particular \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) vanishes
1083	  iff \(H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for every \(V\) in a basis of
1084	  the topology of \(S\); since the affine opens form such a basis, it suffices to
1085	  check the vanishing on affine \(V\). This is the local-on-the-base description
1086	  through which \(f_*\)-acyclicity is reduced to absolute cohomology vanishing on
1087	  preimages.
1088	\end{lemma}
1089	
1090	\begin{proof}
1091	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
1092	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
1093	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. By definition \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is the \(k\)-th
1094	  cohomology sheaf of the complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\); since the abelian
1095	  structure on \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules computes cokernels and kernels objectwise on
1096	  the level of presheaves and then sheafifies, that cohomology sheaf is the
1097	  sheafification of the presheaf \(V \mapsto H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)
1098	  = H^k\bigl(\mathcal{I}^\bullet(f^{-1}(V))\bigr) = H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\),
1099	  the last equality because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is an injective (hence
1100	  acyclic) resolution computing the absolute cohomology of \(f^{-1}(V)\). Evaluation
1101	  of a sheafification vanishes exactly when the underlying presheaf vanishes on a basis,
1102	  giving the stated affine-local criterion.
1103	\end{proof}
